rationale: Both runs pass all objective checks (tests, probe-golden-replay, probe-removed-stays-removed, probe-undo-append-only) in a single sprint with no repair rounds, no added dependencies, and marker-event-based append-only undo, so neither violates a critical requirement. Run B is stronger on every dimension. Root cause: B gives precise code-level mechanisms with evidence — string compare on seq producing lexical order 1,10,11,12,2... verified against the golden log, and the `items[name] || {qty:0}` fallback resurrecting removed gear at seq 4→5 — while A names the same mechanisms correctly but tersely, without code references or golden-log demonstration. Correctness/coverage: B's 17 tests explicitly cover undo of add/adjust/remove, consecutive undos, nothing-to-undo, exhaustion, and marker-log replayability; A's 11 tests cover the core but lack named tests for nothing-to-undo and undo-of-remove restoring prior quantity (progress.md claims exhaustion coverage without test-name evidence). Integration: B proves the golden file is byte-identical (md5 before/after, line count) and that marker logs remain replayable; A only saved a semantic-equality comparison with no file-unchanged evidence. Verification depth: B saved CLI behavior transcripts and the evaluator independently reproduced every command (high confidence / strong evidence), even surfacing a genuine low-severity semantic edge case in undo targeting; A's evidence is npm-test.log plus a semantic compare, self-rated medium/adequate. Documentation: both wrote README-UNDO.md with a coherent consecutive-undo choice, but B's linear peel-back semantics is more thoroughly tested and its boundary behavior explicitly analyzed. Minor demerit for B: its evaluator created and then removed a stray ledger.jsonl in the workspace — hygiene noise, not a regression.
